chunk_summary: Early initiation of exclusive breastfeeding within the first hour after birth, prioritizing skin-to-skin contact, supports infant health, maternal well-being, and long-term benefits. Exclusive breastfeeding (EBF) for six months is essential, with on-demand feeding guided by hunger cues and avoiding disruptive practices common in India.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of initiating breastfeeding within the first hour after birth and the role of colostrum.
Learn proper positioning and latch techniques to optimize milk transfer and reduce nipple trauma.
Recognize the benefits of exclusive breastfeeding for six months, including infection prevention and maternal health protection.
Identify cultural practices that disrupt EBF and counsel families on maintaining breastfeeding.
key_facts: Breastfeeding should be initiated within the first hour after birth to trigger rooting and oxytocin release.
Exclusive breastfeeding (EBF) means feeding only breast milk for the first six months, with no water, honey, or other supplements unless medically indicated.
Newborns typically feed 8–12 times per 24 hours, with cluster feeding being common.
Night feeds are crucial for maintaining milk supply due to prolactin peaks overnight.
EBF reduces diarrheal and respiratory infection risks, supports neurodevelopment, and lowers maternal cancer risks.
Breast milk is ~88% water and meets hydration needs, even in hot weather.
reference_quote: Exclusive breastfeeding for six months lowers diarrheal and respiratory infection risks, supports neurodevelopment, and protects maternal health. (Source: World Health Organization)
document_key_topics: Cultural challenges to breastfeeding in India
